short: A joining word that wires clauses or sentences together and signals the logical relation between them: cause, result, contrast, time, or sequence.
long: Connectives (also called linkers or connectors) are the traffic signals of a text: perché/siccome announce a cause, quindi/perciò a result, ma/però/invece a contrast, prima di/dopo che a time relation, innanzitutto/poi/infine the order of points. Choosing between connectives is rarely about meaning alone; position (siccome opens, perché follows), register (poiché vs visto che) and the verb form they govern (benché + subjunctive, prima di + infinitive) all pick the winner.

short: A connective that concedes an obstacle before pushing past it: although, even though, despite. Italian's concessives each demand a fixed verb form.
long: A concessive clause admits something that might have blocked the main statement (Although it was late, he went out). Italian's concessives are a matched set: anche se takes the indicative, benché/sebbene/nonostante take the subjunctive, pur takes the gerund, and nonostante/malgrado can take a bare noun. The meaning is near-identical across the set; the machinery is what differs, so the verb form on the page effectively chooses the connective for you.

short: The formality level a word carries: the difference between visto che (chat), siccome (neutral) and poiché (reports and essays), all meaning because.
long: Register is the social dress code of vocabulary. Many Italian connectives come in register ladders with identical meaning: visto che / dato che (conversational) up to poiché / giacché (written-formal) for cause; quindi (neutral) up to pertanto (bureaucratic) for result; però (speech) beside tuttavia (essays) for contrast. A register mismatch is style rather than grammar, so Linguics items that hinge on register say so in the prompt and grade mismatches gently rather than flagging them wrong.

short: A signpost word that orders a text: innanzitutto opens, poi carries on, infine closes, insomma sums up.
long: Sequencers (discourse markers of ordering) do not join two clauses grammatically; they organise whole stretches of text. Italian's core set: innanzitutto / prima di tutto (first of all), poi / in seguito (then), inoltre / per di più (moreover), infine (finally, closing a series), insomma / in conclusione (summing up). Classic traps: infine vs allora (a sequence-closer is not a consequence-drawer) and inoltre vs oltre a (free-standing moreover vs a leaning word that must rest on a noun or infinitive).
